coredata.dc:title: Extreme gradient boosting-enhanced molecularly imprinted fluorescence nanosensor for rapid identification and visual detection of deltamethrin in seawater and aquatic products
coredata.dc:creator: You, Jinjie
Hao, Guijie
Gan, Xintian
Chen, Rui
Chen, Yuge
Zhang, Zeming
Sun, Aili
Liu, Hua
Shi, Xizhi
coredata.dc:description: Deltamethrin (DEL), a synthetic pyrethroid insecticide, may have long-term adverse effects on the aquatic environment. Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) is a classic ensemble boosting algorithm framework known for its high training efficiency, strong prediction performance, and versatile applications. In this study, we successfully developed a highly sensitive molecularly imprinted fluorescence nanosensor (MF-sensor) for DEL determination, enhanced by the XGBoost algorithm. The MF-sensor was fabricated by grafting blue and red CdSe/ZnS quantum dots onto a molecularly imprinted silica layer. The MF-sensor consisted of blue-molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP)-quantum dots (QDs) and r-MIP-QDs, which selectively detected DEL, and unmodified green CdSe QDs, which were not selective for DEL. Under optimized conditions, we observed an excellent linear relationship between the I 526/(I 450 +I 630) ratio and DEL concentration ranging from 0.01 mg/L to 40.0 mg/L (R2 = 0.9944). The limit of detection was determined to be 1.34 µg/L. The recoveries in actual samples ranged from 98.5% to 109.0%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) below 7.5%. Additionally, we utilized the XGBoost algorithm to establish a DEL prediction model, achieving an accuracy of 95.7%. The recoveries ranged from 96.0% to 102.4%, with a RSD below 5.5%. Overall, the proposed MF-sensor, enhanced by the XGBoost algorithm, was successfully utilized for the detection of DEL in environmental and aquatic products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
MIP-QDs
Visual detection
Deltamethrin
XGBoost

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurements of fluorescent response to DEL
Optosensing of DEL by MIP-QDs under different solvents
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the MF-sensor
Practical applicability and XGBoost training of the MF-sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs
Ningbo Municipal Bureau of Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Public Welfare Research Project of Ningbo

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Open Research Fund of Key Laboratory of Healthy Freshwater Aquaculture, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Afairs ( ZJK202205 )，the Public Welfare Research Project of Ningbo ( 2022S163 )，the Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau ( 2021Z102 ), the " Pioneer" and "Leading Goose" R&D Program of Zhejiang ( 2023C02038 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, B) SEM images, (C, D) TEM images, (E, F) EDS energy spectra, (G) hydrodynamic diameter, (H) XPS energy spectra, and (I) FT-IR images of MIP-QDs and NIP-QDs. Inset (a, b) shows the high-resolution SEM of MIP-QDs and NIP-QDs.
Effects of (A) the concentration of MIP-QDs, (B) percentage of water in acetonitrile, (C) salinity of the system, and (D) acetic acid of the system on the fluorescence response of MIP/NIP-QDs.

Fluorescence spectra of the MF-sensor for DEL detection from 0.01 mg/L to 40 mg/L, which contained g-CdSe QDs (10%), r-MIP-QDs (3.5%), and (A) 10.0%, (B) 9.5%, (C) 9.0%, (D) 8.5%, (E) 8.0%, and (F) 7.5% b-MIP-QDs. The upper insets give the corresponding fluorescence images under a 365 nm UV lamp.
(A) Selectivity and (B) competitiveness of the MF-sensor (inset shows images captured under a 365 nm UV light).

Fluorescence spectra of (A) MF-sensor and (B) Mix-NIP-QDs under different deltamethrin concentrations (Inset shows images taken under 365 nm UV light). (C) XGBoost model construction, (D) concentration chromaticity diagram and rapid detection equipment model.
The determination of DEL in aquatic products and seawater samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Extreme gradient boosting-enhanced molecularly imprinted fluorescence nanosensor for rapid identification and visual detection of deltamethrin in seawater and aquatic products

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products, School of Marine Sciences, Ningbo University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Deltamethrin (DEL), a synthetic pyrethroid insecticide, may have long-term adverse effects on the aquatic environment. Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) is a classic ensemble boosting algorithm framework known for its high training efficiency, strong prediction performance, and versatile applications. In this study, we successfully developed a highly sensitive molecularly imprinted fluorescence nanosensor (MF-sensor) for DEL determination, enhanced by the XGBoost algorithm. The MF-sensor was fabricated by grafting blue and red CdSe/ZnS quantum dots onto a molecularly imprinted silica layer. The MF-sensor consisted of blue-molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP)-quantum dots (QDs) and r-MIP-QDs, which selectively detected DEL, and unmodified green CdSe QDs, which were not selective for DEL. Under optimized conditions, we observed an excellent linear relationship between the I 526/(I 450 +I 630) ratio and DEL concentration ranging from 0.01 mg/L to 40.0 mg/L (R2 = 0.9944). The limit of detection was determined to be 1.34 µg/L. The recoveries in actual samples ranged from 98.5% to 109.0%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) below 7.5%. Additionally, we utilized the XGBoost algorithm to establish a DEL prediction model, achieving an accuracy of 95.7%. The recoveries ranged from 96.0% to 102.4%, with a RSD below 5.5%. Overall, the proposed MF-sensor, enhanced by the XGBoost algorithm, was successfully utilized for the detection of DEL in environmental and aquatic products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Deltamethrin (DEL) is a synthetic pyrethroid II insecticide that is mainly used for the control of terrestrial and aquatic pests [1]. The use of DEL has increased substantially as carbamate/organophosphorus insecticides have been banned in numerous countries [2]. However, DEL is highly stable and can easily result in residues in food, soil, and aquatic environments [3]. Meanwhile, as a neurotoxin, DEL can induce the production of excessive reactive oxygen species in aquatic organisms, affecting their liver function, proteins, and genes [4]. Furthermore, frequent use of DEL can lead to its accumulation in the human body, thereby interrupting sodium channels in axons that causing upper respiratory tract irritation, dizziness, vomiting, and other acute symptoms [5]. Currently, numerous methods for detecting DEL, including liquid chromatography (LC) [6], enzyme-linked immunosorbent [7], electrochemical sensor [8], fluorescence sensor [9], have been reported. LC has the advantages of high precision and good stability [8]. However, its complex pretreatment steps, long detection cycle, and high sample consumption greatly limit its application in rapid detection. Electrochemical and fluorescence sensors can detect DEL qualitatively and semi-quantitatively according to changes in electrical or fluorescent signals. However, traditional detection methods exhibit single-signal capability, poor self-calibration performance, and limitations in field rapid detection [10]. Therefore, establishing a fast and accurate method for detecting DEL residues in aquatic ecosystems is of great significance.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are biomimetic materials synthesized using molecular imprinting technology [11]. MIPs exhibit excellent selective recognition performance due to their controllable binding sites [12]. Common MIPs preparation methods include surface imprinting, precipitation polymerization, and reversed-phase microemulsion, each exhibiting different performance characteristics. Surface imprinting reduces mass transfer resistance and shortens detection time [13]. Precipitation polymerization creates a large specific surface area, resulting in outstanding adsorption capacity and an extended detection range [14]. The reversed-phase microemulsion method is a new imprinting technique that combines the advantages of surface imprinting and precipitation polymerization, thereby offering good stability and controllability, holding great potential in MIPs preparation [15]. The MIP-QDs based fluorescence sensor combines the excellent selectivity of MIPs with the optical properties of quantum dots (QDs). They achieve outstanding results in detecting biotoxins, such as aflatoxin and okadaic acid, as well as antibiotics, such as tetracycline and chloramphenicol [16,17].

However, single-emission fluorescence sensors are susceptible to background noise and human error [18]. Multiple fluorescence sensors with two or more fluorescence signals exhibit excellent self-correction capabilities and offer rich color windows, enabling highly sensitive detection of pollutants using fluorescence signal ratios and colors [19,20]. Multimodal fluorescence sensors offer excellent self-correction capabilities and a rich color window, rendering them more suitable for visual detection compared to single-emission fluorescence sensors. However, despite their advantages, multimodal fluorescence sensors are less commonly used in pesticide residue detection. Visual detection requires a high diversity of color windows [20]. To ensure detection sensitivity, fluorescence color windows must be enriched as much as possible. Therefore, tri-emission fluorescence sensors must be fabricated using the red-green-blue (RGB) strategy. However, for multimodal fluorescence sensors, the corresponding RGB strategy and target pollutant concentration no longer have a simple linear relationship. Additionally, the accurate concentration of the target pollutant cannot be determined by simple visual observation [21]. To address this problem, regression prediction models based on machine learning algorithms have emerged.

Common machine learning algorithms can be categorized into unsupervised and supervised learning. Unsupervised learning reduces the complexity of data to a great extent but may lead to a substantial loss of feature dimensions and require extensive data analysis [22]. Supervised learning, which is widely used in artificial intelligence, risk prediction, and other fields, can strengthen the mining of data and features compared with unsupervised learning and has high model efficiency and performance [23]. Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) is a classic ensemble boosting algorithm framework known for its high training efficiency, strong predictive performance, versatile and controllable parameters, as well as user-friendly characteristics. It stands as a powerful tool in the field of big data analytics, enhancing the gradient boosting decision tree model by adding strategies, such as the automatic optimization of segmentation nodes and handling missing feature values. These additions further enhance the performance and effectiveness of a model [24,25]. Compared with traditional algorithms, the sparse sensing algorithm used by XGBoost algorithm can support the unreliability of hardware sensors [24]. Therefore, the integration of the XGBoost algorithm with the RGB strategy of multimodal fluorescence sensors not only enables the extraction of multifeature parameters and the creation of a unified regression prediction model but also facilitates the rapid prediction of concentrations for unknown samples excluded from the real sample data set. To the best of our knowledge, the application of the XGBoost algorithm to the prediction of RGB strategies for molecularly imprinted multimodal fluorescence sensors has not been reported.

Therefore, we propose an XGBoost-enhanced molecularly imprinted fluorescence nanosensor (MF-sensor) that can detect DEL with excellent selectivity and self-correction capability. The MF-sensor exhibits a wide range of fluorescence changes, transitioning from green to purple. Additionally, we select the RGB feature parameters of the MF-sensor and combine them with the XGBoost algorithm to develop a regression model. This model can specifically detect DEL in seawater and aquatic products, thereby successfully predicting the concentration of DEL residue. The novelty of this study lies in the construction of a tricolor multimodal fluorescence sensor, the use of the RGB strategy in regression prediction with the XGBoost algorithm, and the establishment of a new scheme for predicting DEL residues in an instrument-free environment. These findings provide theoretical support for the development of intelligent and rapid detection instruments for DEL.

Cypermethrin (CYP), DEL, bifenthrin (BIF), phenothrin (PHE), and cyfluthrin (CYF) were obtained from Shanghai Pesticide Research of China. Triton X-100 was purchased from Solarbio Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, 99%) and (3-Aminopropyl) and triethoxysilane (APTES, 99%) were purchased from SigmaAldrich (Steinheim, Germany). CdSe QDs (emission wavelengths of 526 nm) and CdSe/ZnS QDs (emission wavelengths of 450 and 630 nm) were obtained from Beida Jubang Science and Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ammonium hydroxide (25 wt%), acetonitrile, cyclohexane, and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Ultraviolet (UV) absorption was observed with a UVvisible spectrophotometer (UV-3300, Mapada, China). The morphology characteristics of polymers were examined using S-4800 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan) and H-7650 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Hitachi Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FT-IR) analysis was processed using FTIR-4700 (Jasco) and S-4800 elemental composition via energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) (Hitachi, Japan).

Typically, 8.0 mL cyclohexane and 2.0 mL Triton X-100 were mixed in a 50 mL flask and stirred for 15 min. Subsequently, 0.5 mg CdSe/ZnS QDs, 60 µL TEOS, and 100 µL ammonium hydroxide were added to the flask, and the mixture was reacted for 2 h in the dark. Meanwhile, the DEL-APTES prepolymerization solution was prepared by mixing and stirring 20 mg/mL DEL dissolved in xylene with 200 µL cyclohexane and 0.15 mmoL APTES for 2 h in the dark. Finally, the DEL-APTES prepolymerization solution was added to the flask and stirred for 12 h in the dark. Subsequently, the prepared MIP-QDs were transferred to a centrifuge tube, and 10 mL acetone was added to terminate the reaction. The mixture was shaken, mixed, left to precipitate, and centrifuged at 8.0 × 103 g for 10 min. After removing the supernatant, 10 mL of water was added for ultrasonic dispersion and centrifugation for 20 min. Finally, the template molecule was eliminated with the mixture of acetonitrile:ethanol (v:v, 2:8) by ultrasonication until no DEL was detected by LC–UV. The non-imprinted QDs polymer (NIP-QDs) was prepared by the same synthesis and elution methods except that the DEL solution was replaced with the same amount of xylene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of fluorescent response to DEL
ce:section.ce:para: The intensity of MIP-QDs fluorescence was recorded by using an F-4600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The excitation wavelength for fluorescence detection was 340 nm, and the emission range from 400–670 nm was recorded. The` excitation and emission gap widths were 5 and 10 nm, respectively, and the photomultiplier tube voltage was 750 eV. The fluorescence intensity of the dual-color/tricolor system was recorded by Varioskan Flash (Thermo Fisher Scientific, 3001/3001–1193). The excitation wavelength and the scanning range were set to 340 nm and 400–670 nm, respectively. The slit width was set to 5 nm. Fluorescence scans were performed thrice in all experiments.

The fluorescence quenching efficiency was evaluated using the Stern–Volmer equation: F0/F= 1 +Ksv[Q], where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of MIP-QDs without the template, F is the fluorescence intensity of MIP-QDs after incubation with the template for 5 min, and [Q] is the concentration of DEL added. The initial fluorescence ratio of the MF-sensor, denoted as Imix (Imix=I526/[I450 +I630]), was recorded. I526, I450, and I630 were divided into the intensities of the MF-sensor at 526, 450, and 630 nm, respectively. The values of the imprinting factor (IF) were calculated based on the Ksv ratio of MIP-QDs and NIP-QDs.

To evaluate the selectivity of MF-sensor, the DEL and structural analogues (PHE, CYP, CYF and BIF) were selected for experiments. Standard solutions of DEL (1 mg/mL) and structural analogues (3 mg/mL) were configured with acetonitrile as solvent. DEL and structural analogues were diluted to appropriate concentration (20 mg/L DEL and 60 mg/L structural analogues) with mixture of acetonitrile and water in subsequent experiments. The test was repeated thrice in each group. Moreover, the combinations of DEL and PHE, DEL and CYP, DEL and CYF, and DEL and BIF were selected for competitive inhibition experiments to verify the specific fluorescence response of the MF-sensor to DEL. F0/F and IF were used as evaluation indexes to verify the selectivity of the MF-sensor.

The seawater samples were filtered with a 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove particulate matter and microorganisms. The samples were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for later use. Fresh aquatic products were homogenized (10 min), weighed to 1.00 ± 0.01 g homogenized aquatic products in 10 mL centrifuge tubes, added with 3 mL acetonitrile and 0.3 g NaCl, and ultrasonicated for 20 min. After centrifuging at 7.0 × 103 g for 5 min, the supernatants were collected and extracted again after swirling for 2 min. Finally, the supernatants were dried under nitrogen and dissolved by the addition of 1.0 mL acetonitrile. The dissolved solutions were filtered through a 0.22 µm nylon membrane and stored at 4 °C for subsequent analysis.

A digital camera was used to capture images under a 365 nm UV lamp. The actual fluorescence colors were then extracted using a self-provided RGB extractor to construct the data set. The data set contained the RGB values of 835 samples and their corresponding concentrations. After selecting the data set, the concentration and characteristic data (RGB) were mined and analyzed using Python 3.9.7 (Windows 10 system and a 2.5 GHz Intel Core i5–7300HQ processor) on the XGBoost model. A predictive concentration RGB model was established, and the correlation coefficient R and root mean square error (RMSE) were used as indicators to evaluate the model performance. The test and training data accounted for 30% and 70% of the data set in the experiment, respectively. The RGB extraction code and corresponding function source code can be found in Texts S1 and S2, respectively.

To overcome the limitations of single-emission fluorescence sensors, we simultaneously prepared two MIP-QDs with different fluorescence emissions. The amounts of functional monomer and crosslinking agent affected the selectivity and fluorescence response of MIP-QDs to the template [26]. As shown in Fig. S1, when the molar ratio of DEL to APTES was 8:1 and the amount of TEOS was 60 µL, MIP-QDs exhibited the highest fluorescence response (F0/F, 1.25) to DEL and the highest IF of 2.33, showing that MIP-QDs had specific fluorescence response to DEL. Subsequently, SEM showed the evident spherical morphology of MIP-QDs with an average size of 80 nm ( Fig. 1A and 1B).

Meanwhile, MIP-QDs had a remarkable imprinted layer under high-resolution SEM, and the NIP-QDs were smooth and spherical (Fig. 1a and b). TEM revealed the notable shell–core structures and rougher surface of MIP-QDs than those of NIP-QDs, which indicated that the imprinted cavities formed on the QDs surface (Fig. 1C and D). Moreover, EDS showed that MIP-QDs and NIP-QDs contained the same elements (Fig. 1E and F, respectively). Specifically, Si was attributed to TEOS and APTES, Cd and Se originated from CdSe QDs, Pt was detected from gold spray-on, and N was traced back from APTES. Additionally, the hydrodynamic diameter of the prepared MIP-QDs was approximately 120 nm in the solution (Fig. 1G). This result indicated the polydispersion of MIP-QDs [27]. The elemental composition of MIP-QDs and NIP-QDs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as illustrated in Fig. 1H). The XPS results showed the peaks corresponding to Si, Si2 s, Se3 s, Cd3d5/2, O, O loss, and O Auger at 2.103, 151, 195, 405, 532, 553, and 980 eV, respectively. These peaks revealed the presence of Si, Cd, O, and Se on the surface of CdSe/ZnS QDs. The Zn2p peak at 1025 eV was due to Zn from the zinc sulfide shell. The results confirmed the core/shell–shell structure of CdSe/ZnS-SiO2 of MIP-QDs. Moreover, the findings of the full spectrum scanning of NIP-QDs were not considerably different from those of MIP-QDs, which indicated the elution of the DEL template molecules.

Moreover, FT-IR was used to study the characteristic groups of MIP-QDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1I, the absorption peak of 3430 cm−1 represents the N-H bond, suggesting that the characteristic groups in APTES have been successfully grafted onto QDs, and the absorption peak at 2940 cm−1 denotes the stretching vibration of C-H and N-H bonds. The absorption peaks at 1630 cm−1 are -CO-NH- diamide bonds, which were attributed to the aminopropyl and amide bonds (-CO-NH-) and bending vibrations of N-H and C-N at 1550 cm−1 and indicate the presence of amino-propyl groups in the material. The sharp absorption peaks at approximately 1070 cm−1 corresponds to those of Si-O-Si bonds, and the absorption peaks at 788 cm−1 and 463 cm−1 represented Si-O bonds. These characteristic chemical bonds and groups indicated that the blotting layer that contained APTES successfully coated the QDs [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optosensing of DEL by MIP-QDs under different solvents
ce:section.ce:para: An adequate interaction between the imprinting cavities of MIP-QDs and template molecules were required to realize specific fluorescence quenching. After the addition of 2.0 mg/L DEL, the fluorescence intensities of the MF-sensor decreased gradually and reached equilibrium at 300 s (Fig. S2). Hence, in the follow-up experiment, the optimal response time was set as 300 s

In general, the concentration of MIP-QDs had a remarkable influence on the linear range and sensitivity of detection; thus, the optimization of the MIP-QDs concentration is an important step to improve their fluorescence response [29]. As shown in Fig. 2A, when the MIP-QDs concentration in the system reached 20 mg/L, the fluorescence response and quenching efficiency of MIP-QDs toward DEL reached the maximum. Therefore, the detection system was constructed, and the 20 mg/L MIP-QD concentration was selected.

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2B, the specificity of MIP-QDs for DEL increased correspondingly with the increase in water proportion. When water proportion reached 40%, the sensor’s response to DEL reached the maximum, and the highest IF of 2.6 was obtained. When the water proportion exceeded 40%, the sensor’s response to the template decreased rapidly. This phenomenon may be due to the weak acid ions ionized in water that can greatly affect the imprinting environment and sites on the polymer surface; the existence state and solubility of template molecules in an aquatic environment are also key factors that affect the imprinting effect [30]. Therefore, based on the aforementioned analysis, a system with a 40% water proportion was selected to optimize the salt concentration.

The salinity within the system interfered with the charge distribution on the surface of MIP-QDs to a certain extent, thereby affecting its specific fluorescence quenching efficiency (Fig. 2C). When the salinity of the system exceeded 5‰, the imprinting effect of the sensor remarkably declined. Environmental ions can accumulate on the MIP-QDs surface and further hinder the binding of the template to MIP-QDs [31]. Therefore, a salinity of 5‰ was selected for subsequent experiments.

DEL is unstable and prone to hydrolysis under alkaline conditions. Thus, the establishment of an acidic environment has a certain promoting effect on improving the specific response of MIP-QDs. When 1% acetic acid was introduced into the system, the degree of fluorescence quenching of MIP-QDs was increased by the template, and with the increased proportion of acetic acid, the inhibition capability of NIP-QDs was greatly improved, indicating the reduced specific recognition capability of the system to the template (Fig. 2D). When the proportion of acetic acid was maintained at 1%, the value of IF reached the highest (2.998). Therefore, a specific response system for DEL was developed under acetonitrile/water (v/v, 6:4) condition, a salinity of 5‰, and an acetic acid ratio of 1%.

The stability of MIP-QDs was further investigated. Although the fluorescence intensity of MIP-QDs decreased after 60 days of repeated detection, the analysis showed no considerable changes in its fluorescence quenching efficiency, which reached more than 90% of the initial state (Figs. S3A and S3B). The results showed that the constructed sensor retained a high specific recognition capability for the template over a long period. Meanwhile, five batches of polymers were prepared under the same conditions, and the prepared MIP-QDs and NIP-QDs were eluted multiple times (Figs. S3C and S3D). The five batches of MIP-QDs synthesized using the same method exhibited identical recognition capability for the template, with all IF values exceeding 2.8. Nevertheless, after undergoing five elution cycles, the sensor’s capability to recognize the template decreased, probably because multiple elution combinations destroyed the imprinting sites on the MIP-QDs imprinting layer. As a result, the number of recognition sites and imprinting efficiency decreased. The results revealed that under the same conditions, the prepared MIP-QDs demonstrated high stability, reproducibility, and a notable ability to be reused for not more than three times.

The appropriate proportion of blue-MIP-QDs (b-MIP-QDs), unmodified green CdSe QDs (gCdSe QDs), and red-MIP-QDs (r-MIP-QDs) is a key factor for enhancing the color richness of the MF-sensor. This ratio plays an important role in obtaining a wide range of color variation and data. Therefore, the ratio of these three factors had a great influence on sensitivity toward DEL and the color abundance of the MF-sensor. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the gCdSe QDs fluorescence intensity of gCdSe QDs remained stable with the increase in DEL concentration. Moreover, the fluorescence color and intensity of gCdSe QDs did not change significantly. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S5, the ratios of gCdSe QDs to r-MIP-QDs were first optimized. To achieve the best DEL residual visualization, we first fixed the concentration of gCdSe QDs to attain a bright green appearance under a 365 nm UV lamp. Subsequently, the r-MIP-QDs concentration was adjusted to determine the optimal test proportional concentration for visualization. The fluorescence intensity of r-MIP-QDs decreased when different DEL concentrations were added, and the dual-color fluorescence sensor gradually changed from red to orange yellow and finally to green. When the initial volume fraction of r-MIP-QDs exceeded 3.5%, the dual-emission ratio fluorescence sensor lost the green portion under the 365 nm UV lamp and only achieved the light-yellow effect. However, when the initial volume fraction of r-MIP-QDs was lower than 3.5%, a weak red light was observed under UV lamp irradiation, which narrowed the color range of the sensor. Therefore, the proportional concentration of gCdSe QDs and r-MIP-QDs of the dual-emission ratio fluorescence test system was determined to be 10.0%:3.5% (v/v).

Next, b-MIP-QDs were added to build the MF-sensor ( Fig. 3). With the increase in DEL concentration, when the volume fraction of b-MIP-QDs remained at 8.5%, the color of MF-sensor can gradually change from purple to orange-red to orange-yellow to yellow-green and finally gradually changed to light green. When the initial volume fraction of b-MIP-QDs exceeded 8.5%, the color of b-MIP-QDs was blocked, and the orange-red color disappeared, which resulted in a decreased visual color abundance. When the initial volume fraction of b-MIP-QDs was lower than 8.5%, the color display of b-MIP-QDs became incomplete. As a result, the original purple color was lost, and the color change range of the MF-sensor narrowed. Therefore, the final optimal initial volume fraction configuration of gCdSe QDs, r-MIP-QDs, and b-MIP-QDs in the MF-sensor was 10.0%:3.5%:8.5% (v/v/v).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the MF-sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the selective fluorescence quenching mechanism of MIPs on the template material, we performed UV scanning on MIP/NIP-QDs, QDs, and DEL at 200–800 nm. As shown in Fig. S5, CdSe/ZnS QDs exhibited an absorption peak at 340 nm UV. Next, the characteristic peak of DEL appeared at 278 nm, and the absorption peaks of DEL and QDs did not overlap; thus, the fluorescence quenching caused by energy resonance transfer can be excluded [32,33]. Meanwhile, the UV absorption peak of DEL and the fluorescence emission of QDs did not overlap; thus, internal filtration is not the main reason for DEL causing the quenching of the fluorescence of MIP-QDs [34,35]. MIP-QDs and NIP-QDs were covered with the same QDs, consequently exhibiting absorption peak at the UV band of 340 nm. The recognition of templates by MIPs relies on the force between noncovalent bonds, and the Meisenheimer complex formed between the -NH2 provided by APTES and the CO provided by DEL further enhanced the interaction between the template and the imprinted layer; as a result, the template was adsorbed on the imprinted layer [36]. When the electrons in QDs were excited, free charged particles were produced, and when the excited electrons returned to ground state, fluorescence was produced. However, when the free excited electrons contacted the DEL adsorbed in the imprinting site, they occupied the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of DEL and cannot transfer to the ground state, which resulted in the quenching of QDs fluorescence [37]. Overall, photoinduced electron transfer was considered the main cause of MIP-QDs fluorescence quenching after energy resonance transfer, and the internal filtration effect was excluded.

To evaluate the selectivity of MF-sensor, the DEL (20.0 mg/L) and analogues (PHE, CYP, CYF and BIF 60.0 mg/L) were selected for experiments (the structural of analogues were shown in Fig. S7). After the addition of DEL, the the fluorescence intensity of the MF-sensor was inhibited, but the addition of analogues had no effect on the fluorescence of MF-sensor and color hardly changed at 365 nm UV ( Fig. 4 A). The tricolor mixture (Mix-NIP-QDs), which was constructed by NIP-QDs, lacked specific imprinting sites and its fluorescence quenching efficiency (I 450 +I 630)/I 526 was one-third of the MF-sensor. Thus, the prepared MF-sensor showed excellent selective recognition and fluorescence response capability for DEL. To further illustrate the selectivity of the MF-sensor, we included the fluorescence curve in Fig. S8. As presented in Fig. 4B and Fig. S8B, in the presence of other DEL structural analogs, the MF-sensor retained a high recognition capability for DEL, and its color turned light green under a 365-nm UV lamp. However, the Mix-NIP-QDs had no imprinting cavity that could complement the DEL structure, which resulted in almost no change in its color, that is, it remained bright light purple. Moreover, the quenching effect of DEL on Mix-NIP-QDs was lower than that of MF-sensor. These results further prove that the MF-sensor has a specific recognition response to DEL and excellent recognition and selective fluorescence quenching capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical applicability and XGBoost training of the MF-sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The linearity, accuracy, precision, and limits of detection (LODs) of the MF-sensor were measured under optimal conditions, and the practicability of the MF-sensor was investigated. The fluorescence intensity exhibited variation corresponding to different DEL concentrations ( Fig. 5) that the DEL concentration spanned from 0.01 to 40 mg/L. The concentration of DEL exhibits good linear relationship with I 526/(I 450 +I 630) with equation Y= 0.0184X+ 0.4469 (R2 =0.9944). The response standard curve of the MF-sensor to different DEL concentrations (n = 3) is presented. In comparison to Mix-NIP-QDs, the MF-sensor demonstrates excellent DEL recognition, as illustrated in Fig. S9.

Meanwhile, upon irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp, the fluorescence color of the MF-sensor transformed from purple to orange-red to orange-yellow to yellow-green and finally gradually changed to light green with the increasing DEL concentration. However, the color of the Mix-NIP-QDs did not change considerably. Furthermore, utilizing the equation LODs= 3σ/S, which denotes three times the signal-to-noise ratio, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank sample (n = 11), and S is the slope of the standard curve, the LODs values obtained for the developed MF-sensor in seawater and aquatic products were 1.34 µg/L and 2.25 µg/L, respectively.

To further improve the portability and efficiency of detection, we developed the XGBoost machine learning algorithm for the real-time prediction of DEL residues in the environment. Fig. 5C displays the utilization of the self-built RGB extraction model to extract the RGB color from 400 seawater samples and 435 aquatic product samples. Subsequently, the XGBoost algorithm was used to train and fit the RGB values. A total of 70% of the data set was used for training, and 30% was used to predict the training results. The specific values of the parameters used in the XGBoost algorithm were listed in the Supplementary Information and the specific codes are listed in text S2. The results showed that between the values of B and R, the concentration of DEL involved in the prediction reached 66.2% and 33.3%, respectively. Thus, b-MIP-QDs and r-MIP-QDs were the main factors involved in the prediction of DEL, which is consistent with the purpose of preparing the MF-sensor. Crucially, this model achieved a R2 reached 0.9573 and RMSE of 2.34, indicating its excellent prediction capability for DEL. Moreover, it accurately predicts the residual DEL at low concentrations (10.0 µg/L). By using this algorithm instead of the original spectrophotometer measurement, the detection time of DEL was further reduced, and the detection steps were simplified. Furthermore, we proposed a portable UV box that captured the fluorescent color of the MF-sensor (Fig. 5D). This UV box consisted of a display, a tunable wavelength UV lamp, a filter, and a CCD industrial camera. Using the captured MF-sensor fluorescence color, the XGBoost algorithm can accurately predict DEL residues in the environment.

The spiked recoveries of the actual samples were measured in the optimal detection system, and compared with the results determined by UPLC-MS/MS method (related parameters were shown in Supplementary Information, text S3). As shown in Fig. S10, the established UPLC-MS/MS method has excellent detection effect on DEL, with a R2 of 0.9999. DEL was not detected in the actual sample by UPLC-MS/MS prior to the determination of the recovery rate. As shown in Table 1, the recoveries of the MF-sensor in real samples ranged from 98.5–109.0%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 7.5%. The measured results were consistent with the actual spiked concentration and the results of UPLC-MS/MS. For aquatic products and seawater samples, DEL concentrations were serially diluted, resulting in quantitation limits of 15.0 and 5.0 µg/L, respectively. Meanwhile, the XGBoost model, combined with the RGB strategy, predicted DEL residual concentrations through fluorescence imaging. The XGBoost model had an excellent resolution capability for fluorescence images under different DEL concentrations, with recoveries ranging between 96.0% and 102.4%, and an RSD of less than 5.5%. These findings indicated that the MF-sensor with the XGBoost algorithm can remarkably improve accuracy and reduce detection error. Moreover, the multi-directional comparison was made between with the reported sensors and the XGBoost-MF-sensor (Table. S1). The result reflects that this XGBoost-MF-sensor was superior to other sensors in terms of detection range and LOD and exhibited excellent self-calibration function. Meanwhile, instead of naked-eye observation, the developed MF-sensor can distinguish fluorescent colors, improving detection speed and reduces the detection error compared with traditional multimode visual detection methods. These findings demonstrate that the xgboost-based mf sensor exhibits high precision, accuracy, reproducibility, and cost-effectiveness in detecting DEL. As a result, it can effectively replace existing large instruments or paper-based detection methods for DEL detection in seawater and aquatic products.

A MF-sensor combined with the XGBoost machine learning algorithm was successfully fabricated and utilized for the quantitative detection of DEL in real samples. The MF-sensor demonstrated high sensitivity to DEL, displaying modulation in blue and red fluorescence and triggering the activation of green fluorescence, resulting in a diverse range of fluorescent colors. The fluorescence ratio (I 526/(I 450 +I 630)) of the MF-sensor exhibited a favorable linear correlation with DEL concentration within the range of 0.01–40 mg/L (R2 = 0.9944). The obtained LODs for seawater and aquatic products were determined to be 1.34 µg/L and 2.25 µg/kg, respectively. Furthermore, the application of the XGBoost algorithm facilitated efficient classification and prediction of the fluorescence color of the MF-sensor at different DEL concentrations, resulting in substantially improved detection accuracy and speed for DEL. The recovery rates in real samples ranged from 96.0% to 102.4%, with an RSD below 5.5%. In summary, the MF-sensor developed in this study demonstrated exceptional sensitivity, selectivity, reproducibility, and repeatability, making it a reliable tool for DEL detection in the environment. Moreover, the utilization of the XGBoost algorithm provides methodological guidance and a theoretical foundation for the development of subsequent products.

Chen Yuge: Investigation, Validation. Chen Rui: Investigation, Validation. Zhang Zeming: Investigation, Supervision, Validation. Liu Hua: Data curation, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Sun Aili: Investigation, Supervision, Validation. You Jinjie: Data curation, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Shi Xizhi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision. Gan Xintian: Investigation, Validation. Hao Guijie: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Open Research Fund of Key Laboratory of Healthy Freshwater Aquaculture, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Afairs (ZJK202205)，the Public Welfare Research Project of Ningbo (2022S163)，the Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (2021Z102), the "Pioneer" and "Leading Goose" R&D Program of Zhejiang (2023C02038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135355.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jinjie You received his Master’s degree from Ningbo University in 2022. He is currently studying for his PhD at Ningbo University. His research is mainly concerned with the construction of fluorescence and colorimetric sensors and their applications in the environment and organisms.

Guijie Hao received her Dr’s degree from Ningbo University. She is currently a faculty in Zhejiang institute of freshwater fisheries. Her research interests concern on quality safety and processing of aquatic products.

Xintian Gan received her Master’s degree from Ningbo University in 2022. She is currently a faculty in Guizhou center for disease control and prevention. Her research is mainly concerned in molecularly imprinted fluorescence nanosensor and their applications in the food safety.

Rui Chen received his bachelor’s degree from Yibin University in 2021. He is currently studying for his Master’s degree at Ningbo University. His research is mainly concerned with the construction of fluorescence and colorimetric sensors and their applications in the environment and organisms.

Yuge Chen received his bachelor’s degree from Xingzhi College of Zhejiang Normal University in 2021. He is currently studying for a master’s degree at the Marine College of Ningbo University. His main research interests are the construction of molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensors and their applications in the detection of environmental pollutants.

Zeming Zhang received his BS and PhD degree from Ocean University of China in 2013 and 2018. He is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. His research interests concern on the detection, migration and transformation of organicpollutants.

Aili Sun received her Dr’s degree from Ningbo University in 2017. She is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. Her research interests concern on the development and application of fluorescence sensors for food safety.

Hua Liu received her Dr’s degree from University of Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2019. She is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. Her research interests concern on the applications of fluorescent probes and fluorescence sensing in environmental and biological diagnostics.

Xizhi Shi received his Dr’s degree from Shanghai Jiaotong University in 2008. He is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. His research interests concern on the development of fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for bioanalysis.
